narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 55.5% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, like your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and LSAT score of 37, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles, family income in the middle bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. The model flagged your overall profile as higher risk compared to students who typically pass the bar, who often come from stronger academic standings and diverse family backgrounds, with middle-income families making up about a third of passers but usually paired with higher grades. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1, which stands out well below the average of around 6 for those who pass, signaling a notable dip in performance that the model sees as a strong warning sign for bar success. Next in influence, your LSAT score of 37 worked in your favor, as it's quite close to the 37.27 average among passers, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing and showing your potential in that area. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 also helped a bit by nudging things toward success, though it's lower than the 3.24 average for bar passers, softening the overall risk but not enough to offset the top concerns. These three elements—your later-year grades most heavily, followed by LSAT and undergrad GPA—dominated the model's thinking for your specific case. While your full-time status aligns with most passers (over 90%), and your middle family income fits a common profile, they didn't carry enough weight to change the outcome. This isn't a judgment on your character or potential, but a data-driven estimate based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or strengthening your recent academic decile through additional coursework or a post-bac program, then reapply with an updated profile that addresses these key areas. Many students in similar spots have improved their trajectories by targeting these exact factors, and we're here to support your next steps.
